title: SuperBEST v3 Cost Analysis — Acid-Base Chemistry
note_log_base_conversion: log₁₀(x) = (1/ln10)·ln(x). Since 1/ln10 is a free constant c, log₁₀(x) = mul(c, ln(x)) costs 1 ln + 1 mul = 1+2 = 3n. However, when multiplied by another free constant (e.g., −1), the two constants fold into one: −log₁₀(x) = mul(−c, ln(x)) = 1 ln + 1 mul = 3n total (no separate neg needed).

analysis.key_insight: neg and the base-conversion constant fold into a single free constant −1/ln(10). The computation is: mul(−c, ln([H⁺])) where −c = −1/ln(10) is a single terminal constant.

operations.note: −c = −1/ln(10) is a free constant; no separate neg primitive needed

analysis.alternative_rejected: neg(mul(c, ln([H⁺]))) would cost 1+2+2 = 5n — rejected because the constant absorbs the negation

analysis.key_insight: pKa is a free constant terminal. log₁₀([A⁻]/[HA]) = mul(c, ln([A⁻]/[HA])). The ratio [A⁻]/[HA] = mul([A⁻], recip([HA])). The outer add(pKa, …) costs 3.
analysis.note_on_expanded_form: Expanding as log₁₀([A⁻]) − log₁₀([HA]) would require two separate ln + mul pairs plus a sub: 1+2 + 1+2 + 2 = 8n for just the log difference, then +3 for add(pKa,…) = 11n total. The ratio form ln([A⁻]/[HA]) saves 1n.

analysis.key_insight: Ka and Ca are both terminals. Ka·Ca = mul(Ka, Ca). Square root = pow(·, 1/2) costs 3. Total is compact.

operations.note: square root via pow with exponent 1/2

analysis.alternative: exp(mul(0.5, ln(mul(Ka,Ca)))) = 1+2+2+1 = 6n — pow is cheaper when n is a constant

analysis.key_insight: 2.303·C folds into a single free constant k = 2.303·C. Numerator: mul(Ka, [H⁺]). Denominator: pow(add(Ka,[H⁺]), 2). Full expression: mul(k, mul(mul(Ka,[H⁺]), recip(pow(add(Ka,[H⁺]),2)))).
Division by 2 is multiplication by 0.5 (free constant). Ka is a terminal. Need: Ka², 4·Ka·Ca, their sum, its square root, then subtract Ka and scale.

tree.note: 4·Ka is a single folded constant since Ka is a given parameter; however Ka is a variable terminal (not a universal constant), so 4·Ka cannot be precomputed — it requires an explicit mul.

operations.note: 4·Ka·Ca — the 4 is a free constant folded into mul

analysis.note_on_4Ka: If Ka is treated as a domain-specific known constant (fixed per problem), 4·Ka collapses to a single terminal and saves the inner mul(Ka,Ca) restructuring; but in the general case where Ka is an input variable, the mul(4, mul(Ka,Ca)) decomposition is required.

cost_if_Ka_constant.description: When Ka is a fixed constant (e.g., Ka=1.8e-5 for acetic acid), 4·Ka is also a constant terminal. Then mul(4Ka_const, Ca) costs 2n instead of 4n (two muls). Saving: 2n.
cost_if_Ka_constant.breakdown: 3 (Ka²_const via pow, but Ka² also constant → 0!) + ... actually Ka² is also a constant → 0n; mul(4Ka,Ca)=2n; add=3n; sqrt=3n; neg(Ka)=2n; add=3n; ×0.5=2n = 15n
cost_if_Ka_constant.revised_breakdown: Ka² is precomputed constant (0n) + mul(4Ka_const, Ca) = 2n + add(Ka²_const, 4KaCa) = 3n + sqrt = 3n + neg(Ka_const folded) = 0n (absorbed) + add(neg_const, sqrt) = 3n + mul(0.5,…) = 2n → total 13n

analysis.key_insight: γH is a terminal (treated as given). The product γH·[H⁺] = mul(γH, [H⁺]). Then −log₁₀(product) = mul(−c, ln(product)) where −c = −1/ln(10) is a single free constant — identical folding as equation 1.
analysis.comparison_to_eq1: Equation 1 costs 3n (ln + mul) because [H⁺] is a direct terminal. Equation 6 costs 5n because the argument to ln is itself a mul(γH,[H⁺]) rather than a bare variable.

summary_table.name: Quadratic exact [H⁺] (Ka variable)
summary_table.primitives: pow, mul×3, add×2, sqrt, neg, mul

cross_cutting_findings: Constant folding is the dominant cost-reduction lever: neg+base-conversion → single mul constant saves 2n in every pH formula.
log₁₀(x) alone costs 3n (ln + mul×c); −log₁₀(x) also costs 3n because −c absorbs the neg — no extra node.
The quadratic formula is the most expensive primitive at 20n (Ka variable) or 13n (Ka fixed), dominated by two add nodes (3n each) and one pow(·,2) + one pow(·,0.5).
Buffer capacity (14n) is expensive due to the squared denominator: pow(add(…),2) already costs 6n before the outer division.
Henderson-Hasselbalch benefits from the ratio form ln([A⁻]/[HA]) = ln([A⁻]) − ln([HA]) expressed as a single ln after a recip+mul, saving 1n versus expanding into two separate log terms.
